sub_subjects.name: Important Amendments
sub_subjects.description: 1st to 105th · Key changes · Trick to remember

cards.heading: Constituent Assembly — Key Facts
points: Constituent Assembly formed on the recommendation of the Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)
Members: 389 total originally (299 after partition); indirectly elected by Provincial Legislative Assemblies
First meeting: 9 December 1946; Temporary Chairman: Dr. Sachchidanand Sinha; Permanent President: Dr. Rajendra Prasad
Drafting Committee: 7 members; Chairman Dr. B.R. Ambedkar; other key members — Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar, N. Gopalaswami Ayyangar, K.M. Munshi, T.T. Krishnamachari
Time taken: 2 years, 11 months, 18 days; 11 sessions; 165 days of debate
Adopted: 26 November 1949 (celebrated as Constitution Day / Samvidhan Divas since 2015)
Enforced: 26 January 1950 (Republic Day) — chosen because 26 Jan 1930 = Poorna Swaraj Day
Cost of making: ₹64 lakh
Members who signed: 284 members signed on 24 January 1950
cards.alert: ⭐ Ambedkar called it 'more discussed than any constitution in history'. J.B. Kripalani said 'We have accepted the sovereignty of the people'. B.N. Rau = Constitutional Advisor who prepared initial draft. Objective Resolution moved by Nehru on 13 December 1946 = basis of Preamble.

cards.heading: Preamble — Word by Word Analysis
points: WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA — people are sovereign; not gifted by any monarch or outside power
SOVEREIGN — independent; no external authority above India; not a dominion; can acquire or cede territory
SOCIALIST — added by 42nd Amendment 1976; equitable distribution of wealth; mixed economy (not purely Marxist); Mines Nationalisation, Land Reforms

SECULAR — added by 42nd Amendment 1976; no state religion; state does not favor or discriminate based on religion; equal respect for all religions ('Positive secularism' — different from Western 'negative secularism')
DEMOCRATIC — government of the people, by the people, for the people (Lincoln); universal adult franchise; free and fair elections; peaceful transfer of power
REPUBLIC — elected head of state (President); not hereditary monarchy; India chose republic over monarchy
JUSTICE — Social (no discrimination by caste/religion/sex), Economic (equal pay, no exploitation), Political (universal franchise, political equality)
LIBERTY — of thought, expression, belief, faith, and worship; no arbitrary restrictions
EQUALITY — of status and opportunity; no hereditary privilege
FRATERNITY — brotherhood; UNITY AND INTEGRITY OF THE NATION (added by 42nd Amendment 1976) — assuring dignity of the individual
cards.alert: ⭐ Preamble is NOT justiciable (cannot be enforced in courts alone) but SC held it IS part of Constitution (Kesavananda Bharati 1973). Preamble can be amended (Berubari Union case 1960 said NO; but 42nd Amendment 1976 DID amend it — SC accepted). Preamble = 'key to open the minds of the makers' — Granville Austin.

cards.heading: Key Features — All Important for Exam
points: 1. Lengthiest written constitution in the world: Originally 395 Articles + 8 Schedules + 22 Parts; currently 448 Articles + 12 Schedules + 25 Parts
2. Drawn from various sources: 'Bag of borrowings' (K.C. Wheare); borrowed from 10+ constitutions
3. Blend of rigidity and flexibility: Some parts need special majority (Art. 368) + state ratification; some parts by simple majority; ordinary bills = simple majority

4. Federal with unitary bias (quasi-federal): Features of both federation (dual polity, written constitution, division of powers, supremacy of constitution) + unitary (strong centre, single citizenship, emergency provisions, residuary powers with centre)
5. Parliamentary form of government: Executive responsible to legislature; PM as head of government; President = nominal head; collective responsibility (Art. 75)
6. Fundamental Rights: Justiciable; enforceable in courts; Part III (Art. 12–35)
7. Directive Principles: Non-justiciable guidelines for state; Part IV (Art. 36–51)
8. Fundamental Duties: Part IVA (Art. 51A); added by 42nd Amendment 1976; non-justiciable
9. Secular state: No state religion; Art. 25–28
10. Independent Judiciary: Judicial review; SC as guardian of Constitution
11. Universal Adult Franchise: All citizens 18+ can vote (Art. 326); voting age reduced from 21 to 18 by 61st Amendment 1988
12. Single citizenship: Only Indian citizenship (no state citizenship like USA)
13. Emergency provisions: Three types — Art. 352, 356, 360

cards.heading: Borrowed Features — Complete Table

rows: Parliamentary form of government; Rule of Law (Art. 14); Single citizenship; Writs (Habeas Corpus etc.); Bicameral legislature; Speaker in Lok Sabha; Legislative procedure; Cabinet system
Fundamental Rights (Part III); Judicial Review; Preamble (concept); President as Commander-in-Chief; Removal of SC/HC judges (Impeachment process); Written constitution
Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP); Nomination of members to Rajya Sabha by President; Method of election of President (Art. 55)
Federal system with strong Centre; Residuary powers with Centre (unlike USA — with states); Appointment of state governors by President; Advisory jurisdiction of Supreme Court (Art. 143)
Joint sitting of Parliament (Art. 108); Concurrent List (List III of 7th Schedule); Freedom of trade and commerce (Art. 301)
Suspension of Fundamental Rights during National Emergency; Art. 20 cannot be suspended
Amendment procedure by special majority (Art. 368); Nomination of President (Art. 58)
'Procedure established by law' (Art. 21 — due process of law concept)
Fundamental Duties (Art. 51A); Ideal of Justice (social, economic, political) in Preamble
Republic; Liberty, Equality, Fraternity in Preamble; Concept of Republic

cards.alert: ⭐ TRICK to remember: 'UK gave us Parliament; USA gave us Rights; Ireland gave us DPSP; Canada gave us Strong Centre; Australia gave us Joint Sitting; Germany gave us Emergency; South Africa gave us Amendment'. Dr. Ambedkar said Constitution is 'a vehicle of life; as it moves, it must be varied to suit the conditions and times'.

notes.topic_name: Schedules and Parts of the Constitution

cards.heading: 12 Schedules — All for Exam

rows: Names of States and Union Territories
President, VP, Governors, Speakers, Judges, CAG, etc.
For ministers, MPs, MLAs, judges, etc.
Allocation of seats in Rajya Sabha
How many RS seats each State/UT gets
Administration and Control of Scheduled Areas and Tribes
Governors' special powers for tribal areas
Administration of Tribal Areas in NE States
Special provisions for Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, Mizoram
Union List (100), State List (61), Concurrent List (52 subjects)
Land Reform and other Acts (protected from judicial review)
Added by 1st Amendment 1951; SC in Coelho case (2007) held 9th Schedule laws can be challenged if they violate Basic Structure
Added by 52nd Amendment 1985; disqualification for changing party after election
Added by 73rd Amendment 1992; 29 subjects listed for Panchayats
Added by 74th Amendment 1992; 18 subjects listed for Municipalities

cards.alert: ⭐ 7th Schedule most important for exams: Union List (100 subjects — Defence, Foreign affairs, Atomic energy, Railways, Banking, Currency, Census, SC/HC judges appointments); State List (61 subjects — Law+order, Agriculture, Health, Local government, Police); Concurrent List (52 subjects — Education, Forest, Trade unions, Marriage, Criminal law, Drugs). Conflict on Concurrent list: Central law prevails.

cards.heading: Six Fundamental Rights at a Glance

rows: Equality before law; No discrimination; Abolition of untouchability (Art.17); Abolition of titles (Art.18)
6 freedoms (Art.19); Protection against conviction (Art.20); Right to life (Art.21); Right to education (Art.21A); Protection against arbitrary arrest (Art.22)
Prohibition of human trafficking + forced labour (Art.23); Child labour prohibition under 14 years in hazardous work (Art.24)
Freedom of conscience + practice + propagate religion (Art.25); Manage religious affairs (Art.26); No religious taxes (Art.27); No religious instruction in state-aided schools (Art.28)
Protection of minority language+culture (Art.29); Right of minorities to establish+administer educational institutions (Art.30)
Right to move SC for enforcement of Fundamental Rights; Ambedkar called it 'Heart and Soul of Constitution'; Writs available

cards.alert: ⭐ Right to Property (Art. 31) was REMOVED as Fundamental Right by 44th Amendment 1978 — now a legal right under Art. 300A. Currently only 6 Fundamental Rights (after removal of 7th). FRs apply against STATE (Art. 12 defines state). Private individuals cannot be sued for violating FRs (except Art. 17 untouchability and Art. 23 bonded labour).

cards.heading: Articles 14 to 18 — Detailed
points: Art. 14: Equality before law (British concept) + Equal protection of laws (US concept); Rule of Law; No person above law; but reasonable classification allowed (intelligible differentia + rational nexus)

Art. 15: No discrimination by State on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth; BUT allows: special provisions for women + children (Art.15(3)); special provisions for backward classes, SC, ST (Art.15(4)); special provisions for economically weaker sections — 10% EWS (Art.15(6) added by 103rd Amendment 2019)

Art. 16: Equality of opportunity in public employment; no discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, or residence; BUT allows: reservations for backward classes (Art.16(4)); SC/ST reservations (Art.16(4A)); carry-forward rule + consequential seniority (Art.16(4B)); reservation for EWS 10% (Art.16(6) by 103rd Amendment 2019)

Art. 17: Abolition of Untouchability; practicing untouchability = offence; Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955 (now SC/ST Prevention of Atrocities Act 1989) enforces this; untouchability in any form prohibited

Art. 18: Abolition of Titles; no military or academic distinction abolished; but military/academic distinctions allowed (Bharat Ratna, Padma awards = titles? — SC held they are NOT titles under Art.18 since they cannot be used as prefix or suffix to name)
cards.alert: ⭐ Art. 14 = most litigated article in Indian Constitution. 'Equality before law' = negative concept (restraint on government); 'Equal protection of laws' = positive concept (affirmative action allowed). Reasonable Classification test: 1. Intelligible differentia — clear distinction between those classified and those not; 2. Rational nexus — the distinction must be related to the object of the law.

cards.heading: Article 19 — Six Freedoms
points: Art. 19(1)(a): Freedom of speech and expression — includes right to know, right to reply, right not to speak, commercial speech; RESTRICTIONS (Art.19(2)): sovereignty, security, public order, decency/morality, contempt of court, defamation, incitement to offence
Art. 19(1)(b): Freedom to assemble peaceably and without arms — no procession with weapons; restrictions: sovereignty, public order
Art. 19(1)(c): Freedom to form associations or unions or cooperatives (cooperative added by 97th Amendment 2011); restrictions: sovereignty, public order, morality
Art. 19(1)(d): Freedom to move freely throughout the territory of India; restrictions: general public interest, scheduled tribe interests (Art.19(5))
Art. 19(1)(e): Freedom to reside and settle in any part of India; restrictions: same as (d)
Art. 19(1)(g): Freedom to practice any profession or carry on any occupation, trade or business; restrictions: professional qualifications, state monopoly on any trade
ORIGINALLY 7 freedoms: 19(1)(f) — right to acquire, hold and dispose of property was REMOVED by 44th Amendment 1978
cards.alert: ⭐ Art. 19 freedoms available only to CITIZENS (not foreigners). Art. 21 available to ALL PERSONS including foreigners. 19 freedoms suspended during External Emergency (Art. 352) but NOT during Financial Emergency (Art. 360). Arts. 20 and 21 CANNOT be suspended even during National Emergency — 44th Amendment safeguard.

cards.heading: Articles 20, 21, 21A and 22
points: Art. 20: Protection against conviction for offences — 3 protections: (i) Ex-post-facto law — no punishment for act not an offence at the time it was done; (ii) Double jeopardy — no prosecution twice for same offence; (iii) Self-incrimination — no person compelled to be witness against himself

Art. 21: Protection of life and personal liberty — 'No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law' — after Maneka Gandhi case (1978) SC expanded this to include due process; Art. 21 includes: right to live with dignity, right to livelihood, right to health, right to education, right to privacy (KS Puttaswamy case 2017), right to die with dignity (passive euthanasia allowed — Aruna Shanbaug case 2011 + Common Cause case 2018)
Art. 21A: Right to free and compulsory education — added by 86th Amendment 2002; for children 6 to 14 years; RTE Act 2009 gives effect to it

Art. 22: Protection against arbitrary arrest — rights on arrest: (i) right to know grounds of arrest; (ii) right to consult lawyer; (iii) right to be produced before magistrate within 24 hours; (iv) not to be detained beyond 24 hours without magistrate's order; EXCEPTION: preventive detention laws (MISA, NSA, UAPA — allowed)

cards.heading: Articles 23 and 24
points: Art. 23: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour — begar (unpaid labour), traffic in women and children, other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited; punishable offence; state can impose compulsory service for public purposes (army conscription, public schemes) — NOT forced labour

Art. 24: Prohibition of employment of children in factories etc. — no child BELOW 14 YEARS shall be employed in ANY factory, mine, or other hazardous employment; Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Amendment Act 2016 extended this to ALL employment (not just hazardous) for children below 14 years; adolescents 14–18 years cannot work in hazardous occupations
cards.alert: ⭐ Art. 23 applies to BOTH state and private individuals — unusual among Fundamental Rights. Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act 1976 gives effect to Art. 23. Child Labour — exceptions: helping family business; performing arts (with conditions). National Child Labour Policy (1987) — rehabilitation of child labour.

cards.heading: Articles 25 to 28 — Religion Rights
points: Art. 25: Freedom of conscience and free profession, practice and propagation of religion — available to ALL persons (not just citizens); 'propagation' includes spreading one's faith but NOT forcible conversion; restrictions: public order, morality, health; state can regulate: economic/financial/political activities associated with religion; social reform within religion (e.g. Hindu temples open to all Hindus — Art.25(2)(b))

Art. 26: Freedom to manage religious affairs — every religious denomination has right to: establish + maintain religious institutions; manage its own affairs in matters of religion; own + acquire movable/immovable property; administer it in accordance with law

Art. 27: Freedom from payment of taxes for promotion of any particular religion — no one can be compelled to pay taxes for promotion of any religion; BUT charges for specific service (like entry fee to temple) are allowed

Art. 28: Freedom from attending religious instruction — no religious instruction in state-owned schools (completely state-funded); educational institutions using state aid CAN have religious instruction but attendance is voluntary
cards.alert: ⭐ Art. 25 says 'subject to public order, morality and health' — most restrictions on religious freedom. 'Secular' in India means 'equal respect for all religions' not 'separation of religion from state' (unlike USA). Court ruled: wearing of hijab in educational institutions — not protected under Art. 25 (Karnataka HC 2022). Triple Talaq Act 2019 — Art. 25 does not protect practices violating Art. 14 (equality).

cards.heading: Articles 29 and 30 — Minority Rights
points: Art. 29: Protection of interests of minorities — any section of citizens having distinct language, script or culture has right to conserve it; NO citizen shall be denied admission to state-aided educational institutions on grounds of religion, race, caste, or language; available to BOTH minorities and majorities (any section of citizens)

Art. 30: Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions — all minorities (religious OR linguistic) have right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice; state cannot discriminate against minority-run educational institutions while granting aid; government can regulate but not destroy minority character

Who is a minority? No definition in Constitution; determined State by State; Central Govt notified 6 religious minorities: Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists, Zoroastrians (Parsis), Jains (Jains added by 2014 notification)
cards.alert: ⭐ Art. 30(1) available to MINORITY communities (religious or linguistic) only. Art. 29 available to ALL sections including majority communities. Key cases: TMA Pai Foundation case (2002) — HC/SC defined minority. PA Inamdar case (2005) — state cannot impose reservation quotas on unaided minority institutions. RTE Act 2009 — SC in Pramati Educational Trust case (2014) exempted minority institutions from RTE provisions.

cards.heading: Article 32 — Heart and Soul of the Constitution
points: Art. 32: Right to move SC for enforcement of Fundamental Rights — the RIGHT ITSELF is a Fundamental Right
Ambedkar called Art. 32 'the most important article; without it the constitution is null and void; it is the heart and soul of the constitution'
SC can issue 5 types of writs under Art. 32; HC can issue writs under Art. 226 (WIDER jurisdiction — for FR violation + other legal rights)
Art. 32 can be suspended during National Emergency (Art. 359) — BUT Art. 20 and 21 rights cannot be suspended even during Emergency (44th Amendment safeguard)
Art. 32 vs Art. 226: Art. 32 (SC) — only for FR violation; Art. 226 (HC) — for FR violation AND any other legal right; HC writs have wider territorial jurisdiction
cards.alert: ⭐ Difference between Art. 32 and Art. 226: Under Art. 32, SC CAN issue writs; under Art. 226, HC MAY issue writs. Art. 32 is a FR itself so SC cannot refuse — it MUST entertain. Art. 226 is not a FR — HC can exercise discretion. Both can issue all 5 writs.

cards.heading: Five Writs — HMCPQ Trick

rows: Illegal detention; person must be produced before court
Most personal of writs; protects against arbitrary arrest; available against private parties too
Public officials, public bodies, lower courts, government
Compel performance of a public duty that the authority is bound to perform
NOT issued against: President, Governor, Parliament, SC itself, private individuals. Discretionary duty cannot be compelled
Quash order of lower court/authority passed without jurisdiction or in excess of jurisdiction
Both corrective (quash) and preventive; issued by both SC (Art.32) and HC (Art.226)
Prevent lower court from exceeding jurisdiction or acting against natural justice
Preventive only (before order is made); not against executive/administrative authorities; only against judicial bodies
Challenge illegal occupation of public office
Filed by any interested person (not just aggrieved); not available against private offices; requires substantive public office

cards.alert: ⭐ TRICK: H-M-C-P-Q = Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Certiorari, Prohibition, Quo Warranto. Certiorari and Prohibition: Certiorari = curative (issued after order); Prohibition = preventive (before order). Only Habeas Corpus and Quo Warranto available against private persons. Mandamus — most commonly issued writ in India.

topics.name: FR vs DPSP — Conflict and Harmony

cards.heading: What are DPSPs and Their Classification
points: Articles 36–51; Part IV of Constitution; borrowed from the Irish Constitution (in turn from Spanish Constitution of 1937)
Non-justiciable: Cannot be enforced in courts unlike Fundamental Rights; no legal sanction
BUT: Art. 37 says: 'It shall be the duty of the State to apply these principles in making laws; these principles shall be fundamental in the governance of the country'

SOCIALIST principles: Art.38 (welfare state), Art.39 (equal pay, adequate livelihood, children protection), Art.41 (right to work, education, public assistance), Art.42 (maternity relief), Art.43 (living wage), Art.43A (workers participation in management), Art.47 (nutrition and health standards)

GANDHIAN principles: Art.40 (Panchayati Raj), Art.43 (cottage industries), Art.43B (cooperatives — added by 97th Amendment 2011), Art.46 (education of SC/ST/weaker sections), Art.47 (prohibition of intoxicating drinks), Art.48 (cow protection — prohibition on slaughter of cows and calves)

LIBERAL-INTELLECTUAL principles: Art.44 (Uniform Civil Code — UCC), Art.45 (early childhood care and education — now in Art.21A as FR for 6–14 years), Art.48 (modern agriculture and animal husbandry), Art.48A (protection of environment and wildlife — added by 42nd Amendment 1976), Art.49 (protection of monuments), Art.50 (separation of judiciary from executive), Art.51 (promote international peace)
cards.alert: ⭐ Art. 44 — Uniform Civil Code (UCC): 'State shall endeavour to secure a uniform civil code for all citizens.' Among most debated provisions. SC in Shah Bano (1985) and Sarla Mudgal (1995) directed Parliament to enact UCC. Goa has a common civil code (inherited from Portuguese law). Uniform Civil Code Bill — ongoing debate.

rows: Material resources should be distributed to serve common good
Operation of economic system should not result in concentration of wealth
Equal justice and free legal aid
Several states have cow protection laws
Linked to Art. 51A(g) — duty to protect environment
Only partially implemented; magistracy still under executive in many states

cards.heading: 11 Fundamental Duties — Complete List
points: Added by 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act 1976 (based on Sardar Swaran Singh Committee recommendations); originally 10 duties; 11th added by 86th Amendment 2002
Borrowed from: USSR (Soviet Union) Constitution — socialist idea of duties alongside rights
NON-JUSTICIABLE: Cannot be enforced in courts; no punishment for violation (unlike FR violation which is remedied through courts)
BUT Parliament can enact law to make a Fundamental Duty enforceable — Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, etc.
1. Abide by and respect the Constitution, National Flag and National Anthem
2. Cherish and follow the noble ideals which inspired the national struggle for freedom
3. Uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India
4. Defend the country and render national service when called upon
5. Promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood — transcend religious, linguistic, regional or sectional diversities
6. Value and preserve the rich heritage of our composite culture
7. Protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and have compassion for living creatures
8. Develop the scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform
9. Safeguard public property and to abjure violence
10. Strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity
11. (Added by 86th Amendment 2002) — Who is a parent or guardian, to provide opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of 6 and 14 years
cards.alert: ⭐ Duties are in Part IVA (Art. 51A). Verma Committee (1999) on Fundamental Duties recommendations. SC in Ranganatha Reddy case held that duties can be used to interpret ambiguous provisions of FRs. 'A man without duties is like a horse without a bridle' — SC comment on Fundamental Duties.

notes.topic_name: FR vs DPSP — Conflict and Harmony

cards.heading: Evolution of FR vs DPSP Conflict
points: Initial position: FR > DPSP — State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan (1951) — SC held FR superior; Parliament cannot abridge FR to implement DPSP
Parliament's response: 1st Amendment 1951 added 9th Schedule to protect land reform laws from FR challenge
Golak Nath case (1967): SC held Parliament cannot amend Part III (FR); only Constituent Assembly can; Parliament cannot take away or abridge FRs even by Constitutional Amendment
24th Amendment 1971: Parliament reaffirmed its power to amend any part of Constitution including FRs (Art. 13(4) added)

Kesavananda Bharati case (1973): SC held Parliament CAN amend FRs but CANNOT alter the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution; DPSPs are also part of basic structure; FR and DPSP = complementary, not contradictory
42nd Amendment 1976: Added Art. 31C — laws implementing Art. 39(b) and (c) cannot be challenged even if they violate Art. 14 and 19; Minority rule held this cannot extend too far
Minerva Mills case (1980): SC struck down Part of 42nd Amendment; harmony between FR and DPSP is part of Basic Structure; DPSP cannot be given absolute supremacy over FR
CURRENT POSITION: FR and DPSP are complementary; DPSPs guide interpretation of FRs; when conflict, court tries to harmonize; FR has edge but DPSPs given significant weight
cards.alert: ⭐ Basic Structure doctrine (Kesavananda Bharati 1973): 13-judge bench (7:6 majority); components include: Supremacy of Constitution, Republican and democratic form, Secular character, Separation of powers, Federal character, Judicial review, Free and fair elections, Rule of law, Parliamentary system, Harmony between FR and DPSP, Art. 32, Unity and integrity of India.

cards.heading: Parliament — Structure and Key Facts
points: Parliament of India = President + Rajya Sabha (Upper House/Council of States) + Lok Sabha (Lower House/House of the People)
Article 79: Constitution of Parliament
Lok Sabha: Maximum 552 members (530 from States + 20 from UTs + 2 Anglo-Indian nominated — ABOLISHED by 104th Amendment 2020); currently 543 elected seats
Rajya Sabha: Maximum 250 members (238 elected by state legislatures + 12 nominated by President for expertise in arts, literature, science, social service)
Current Rajya Sabha nominees (12): from field of arts, literature, science, social service — NOT politicians by definition
Lok Sabha term: 5 years; can be dissolved by President on PM's advice; extended during Emergency
Rajya Sabha: Permanent body — cannot be dissolved; 1/3 members retire every 2 years; each member serves 6-year term
Quorum: 1/10th of total members for each house (Lok Sabha = 55; Rajya Sabha = 25)

rows: Art. 81 (Constitution of LS); Art. 331 (Anglo-Indian nomination — abolished)
543 elected (now) + any nominated Anglo-Indians before 104th Amendment
Direct election by people of India; FPTP (First Past the Post) system
Speaker (elected by members of LS) + Deputy Speaker (elected by members of LS)
Presides over LS; maintains order; interprets rules; certifies Money Bills; presides over Joint Sitting
Appointed by President when new LS is constituted; senior most member; administers oath to members
25 years minimum age; citizen of India; not holding office of profit; not lunatic or insolvent
5 years from date of first sitting after general election (unless dissolved earlier)
No-confidence motion can only be moved in Lok Sabha (Art. 75); PM must resign if Lok Sabha loses confidence
Can only be introduced in Lok Sabha; Rajya Sabha can only delay for 14 days
Decides whether India is at war (indirectly); confidence motion; annual budget

cards.heading: Rajya Sabha — Detailed Facts

rows: 250 (238 elected by state legislatures + 12 nominated by President)
Indirect — by elected members of State Legislative Assemblies using Single Transferable Vote (Proportional Representation)
Vice President of India (ex-officio Chairman); Deputy Chairman elected by members of RS
Cannot be dissolved; 1/3 retire every 2 years; each member serves 6 years
30 years minimum age; citizen of India; not holding office of profit
Can create new All India Services (Art. 312) by 2/3 majority; can transfer state list subject to Parliament by 2/3 majority (Art. 249)
Cannot introduce; Rajya Sabha cannot amend Money Bill; can only delay 14 days; recommendations not binding on LS
Seats not proportional to population; small states get better representation than in LS

cards.alert: ⭐ Difference: LS represents people; RS represents states. Only LS can pass no-confidence motion. Only RS can authorize Parliament to legislate on State List (Art. 249) or create new All India Service (Art. 312). Joint Sitting (Art. 108) is presided by LS Speaker — NOT applicable to Money Bills and Constitution Amendment Bills.

cards.heading: Sessions, Prorogation and Key Procedures
points: Three main sessions: Budget Session (Feb–May); Monsoon Session (Jul–Aug); Winter Session (Nov–Dec)
Maximum gap between sessions: 6 months (Art. 85(2)); President summons Parliament
Summoning: By President (Art. 85); convened by Speaker/Chairman
Prorogation: Terminates a session (by President); bills pending lapse EXCEPT those passed by one house and pending in other; Presidential bills (Art. 253) do not lapse
Dissolution: Terminates the life of Lok Sabha; only LS is dissolved (not RS)
Question Hour: First hour of sitting every day; 3 types: Starred (oral answer, supplementary questions allowed), Unstarred (written answer, no supplementary), Short Notice (urgent public importance)
Zero Hour: Immediately after Question Hour; matters of urgent public importance; not in Rules of Procedure; Indian invention
Adjournment Motion: Discuss urgent definite public importance; needs 50 members' support; rarely admitted; censures government
Calling Attention Motion: Minister called to make statement on urgent public matter; no vote/debate follows
No-Day-Yet-Named Motion: Listed but no date fixed for debate
Budget: Presented on 1 February (changed from last working day of February in 2017); Vote on Account; Appropriation Bill; Finance Bill
cards.alert: ⭐ Question Hour starts at 11 AM (LS) and 12 Noon (RS). Zero Hour = 12 Noon onwards. Budget Day = 1 February (Arun Jaitley changed in 2017). Joint Sitting (Art. 108): Called by President; presided by LS Speaker; for resolving disagreement between two houses; NOT for Money Bills or Constitution Amendment Bills.

cards.heading: Types of Bills — Ordinary, Money, Finance, Constitution

rows: Either house; needs simple majority in both houses
Equal power — can amend, reject, delay; if disagreement → Joint Sitting (Art.108)
Most common type; 3 readings in each house
Only in Lok Sabha; certified by LS Speaker
Can only delay 14 days; cannot amend; recommendations not binding
Deals with: taxes, appropriation, consolidation fund matters; Speaker's certification = final
Same as Money Bill — 14 days delay only
Contains Money Bill provisions + other matters
EQUAL POWER — not same as Money Bill
Deals with consolidated fund expenditure; both houses equal
EQUAL POWER — both must pass with special majority
2/3 majority present+voting AND majority of total membership; some also need state ratification

cards.alert: ⭐ TRICK for Money Bill: TATVA — Taxes, Appropriation, Treasury (Consolidated Fund), Variation/regulation of borrowing, Audit. If a Bill contains ANY of these provisions AND is certified by Speaker = Money Bill. Joint Sitting cannot resolve Money Bill or Constitution Amendment Bill dispute — only Ordinary Bills.

cards.heading: Special Powers of Parliament and Key Committees
points: Art. 249: RS can authorize Parliament to legislate on State List subject by 2/3 majority (national interest resolution — valid 1 year, renewable)
Art. 312: RS can create new All India Services by 2/3 majority (national interest; existing AIS = IAS, IPS, IFS)
Art. 105: Parliamentary Privileges — freedom of speech in Parliament (cannot be questioned in any court); freedom from arrest in civil cases during session; right to regulate own proceedings

Disqualification of MPs: Art. 102 — office of profit, unsound mind, undischarged insolvent, non-citizen, disqualified by 10th Schedule (Anti-Defection Law); decision by President on EC advice EXCEPT 10th Schedule (Speaker/Chairman decides)

Key Parliamentary Committees: Estimates Committee (1950 — examines budget estimates); Public Accounts Committee (CAG reports — oldest, 1921; chaired by opposition leader by convention); Public Undertakings Committee; Financial Committees = Big Three
Standing Committees: Department-related Standing Committees (24 DRSCs covering all ministries); Committee on Petitions; Committee on Privileges; Business Advisory Committee

Anti-Defection Law (10th Schedule): Added by 52nd Amendment 1985; disqualified if: votes against party whip without exemption; voluntarily gives up party membership; merger = 2/3 members support; Speaker/Chairman decides; SC can review but with limits
cards.alert: ⭐ Parliamentary Privileges — MP cannot be arrested during session for civil matters (can be arrested for criminal/cognizable offence). Parliamentary speech cannot be questioned in court (Subramanian Swamy case etc.). Public Accounts Committee: traditionally chaired by opposition leader by convention (NOT a constitutional requirement). CAG reports go to President → Parliament → PAC.

topics.name: Prime Minister and Council of Ministers

cards.heading: President — Election, Qualification and Powers
points: Art. 52: There shall be a President of India
Art. 53: Executive power of the Union vested in the President; exercised directly or through subordinate officers

Art. 54: Election by Electoral College = Elected MPs (both houses) + Elected MLAs of State Legislatures (not nominated members; not MLCs; not Rajya Sabha nominated); Delhi + Puducherry MLAs also participate (70th Amendment 1992)

Art. 55: Value of votes — uniform scale (parity between states and Centre); each MLA vote value = total population of state ÷ (total elected MLAs × 1000); each MP vote = total value of all MLA votes ÷ total elected MPs
Art. 56: Term — 5 years from date of assuming office; can be re-elected any number of times
Art. 57: Eligible for re-election
Qualification (Art. 58): Citizen of India; 35 years minimum; qualified to be member of Lok Sabha; not holding any office of profit
Salary: ₹5 lakh per month (revised 2018)
Oath administered by: Chief Justice of India (or senior-most SC judge in CJI's absence)

rows: Appoints PM, ministers, governors, CJI, SC judges, CAG, AG, CEC, etc.; can seek information from PM (Art.78)
Summons, prorogues, dissolves Lok Sabha; addresses both houses; nominates 12 RS members + 2 Anglo-Indians to LS (now abolished)
Supreme Commander of Defence Forces; appoints Chiefs of Army, Navy, Air Force
Appoints ambassadors; receives foreign ambassadors; treaties in President's name
Pardoning power (Art.72) — Pardon, Reprieve, Respite, Remit, Commute; advisory jurisdiction (Art.143)
Issue Ordinances when Parliament not in session; same force as law; must be passed by Parliament within 6 weeks of reassembling or lapses
Absolute veto, Suspensive veto, Pocket veto — for ordinary bills; MUST assent to Money Bills and Constitution Amendment Bills

cards.heading: Presidential Veto and Impeachment
points: Absolute Veto: President can refuse assent to ordinary private member bills (government bills cannot be refused as PM has advised assent)

Suspensive Veto: Can return ordinary bill (not Money Bill) to Parliament once for reconsideration; if Parliament passes again = President MUST give assent (unlike USA where 2/3 override needed); 'pocket veto' possible (just delay indefinitely) — as done by President Zail Singh on Postal Amendment Bill 1986
Pocket Veto (no constitutional basis): President can simply not act on a bill indefinitely — no time limit specified in Art. 111 for President to return bill; this was used controversially
For Money Bills and Constitution Amendment Bills: President has NO veto — must give assent

Impeachment (Art. 61): Only for violation of Constitution; charges in either House; 2/3 majority of TOTAL membership of the House passing charges; other House investigates and 2/3 majority to remove; NO impeachment in Indian history so far

Presidential pardon (Art. 72): President can pardon in: death sentence cases (even state cases); court-martial cases; cases of offences against Union law; Governor can pardon (Art. 161) in all cases EXCEPT death sentence + court-martial
cards.alert: ⭐ All 15 Presidents of India: Rajendra Prasad (1st; longest); Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan (2nd; philosopher-statesman); Zakir Husain (3rd; first Muslim; died in office); V.V. Giri (4th; first to be elected as independent candidate); Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed (5th; signed Emergency proclamation); Neelam Sanjiva Reddy (6th; only one elected unopposed — no second term); ...current = Droupadi Murmu (15th; first tribal woman President; elected July 2022).

cards.heading: Vice President — Key Facts
points: Art. 63: There shall be a Vice President of India
Art. 64: VP is ex-officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha
Election (Art. 66): Electoral College = ELECTED + NOMINATED members of BOTH Houses of Parliament (NO MLAs — different from Presidential election)
Qualification: Citizen of India; 35 years minimum; eligible to be member of Rajya Sabha; not holding office of profit
Term: 5 years; no limit on re-election
Removal (Art. 67): By resolution of Rajya Sabha passed by MAJORITY of all members of RS AND agreed to by LS; 14 days' notice required
Acting President: VP acts as President when President is unable to discharge functions due to absence, illness, or vacancy; while acting, VP does NOT perform RS Chairman duties
Salary as RS Chairman: ₹4 lakh per month (revised 2018); no separate salary as VP
cards.alert: ⭐ VP Election: Electoral College includes NOMINATED members too (unlike President's election). Removal of VP: RS alone initiates and passes resolution; LS only agrees — NOTE: For President, BOTH houses initiate impeachment with 2/3 majority. Current VP: Jagdeep Dhankhar (14th VP; elected August 2022).

notes.topic_name: Prime Minister and Council of Ministers

cards.heading: PM and Council of Ministers — Complete
points: Art. 74: Council of Ministers with PM as head shall aid and advise President; President SHALL act on advice (44th Amendment removed 'may' — now mandatory); President CAN return advice once for reconsideration but must accept second advice
Art. 75: Ministers appointed by President on advice of PM; ministers hold office during pleasure of President; Council of Ministers collectively responsible to Lok Sabha (Art. 75(3))

PM: Leader of Council of Ministers; head of government (President = head of state); appointed by President (by convention = leader of majority party/coalition in LS); need not be MP at time of appointment but must become MP within 6 months

Three categories of ministers: Cabinet Ministers (senior; attend Cabinet meetings; heads of major ministries); Ministers of State (MoS) — can be independent charge or attached to Cabinet minister; Deputy Ministers (assist Cabinet minister or MoS; do not attend Cabinet meetings); Parliamentary Secretaries
Cabinet: Smaller body within Council of Ministers; policy-making body; chaired by PM; Cabinet = 'steering wheel of the ship of state'

Collective Responsibility: All ministers must support Cabinet decisions in Parliament and outside; if a minister disagrees, must resign; vote of no-confidence against one minister = resignation of entire Council
Individual Responsibility: Each minister individually responsible for their ministry to Parliament and to PM; can be asked to resign by PM
Cabinet Committees: Cabinet Committee on Security (CCS), Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA), Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs (CCPA), Cabinet Committee on Parliamentary Affairs (CCPA)

cards.heading: Governor — Appointment, Powers and Discretion
points: Art. 153: There shall be a Governor for each State; same person can be Governor of two or more states
Art. 155: Appointed by President (in effect = Central Government's nominee/agent in state)
Art. 156: Holds office at pleasure of President (no fixed term in practice); nominal term = 5 years
Qualification (Art. 157): Citizen of India; 35 years minimum; NOT member of either House of Parliament or state legislature
Art. 163: Council of Ministers headed by CM to aid and advise Governor — EXCEPT for discretionary functions (Art. 163(2))
Art. 164: CM appointed by Governor; other ministers on CM's advice; ministers hold office during pleasure of Governor; CoM collectively responsible to state legislature

DISCRETIONARY POWERS of Governor: Appointment of CM (if no party has clear majority); dismissal of CoM if it loses majority in assembly; dissolution of state legislature on CM's advice; reservation of bills for President's consideration (Art.200); submission of reports to President on state's affairs (Art.356); administering oath to CM and ministers
Governor as Chancellor: Governor is ex-officio Chancellor of state universities (in most states); appoints Vice Chancellors
Art. 356: President's Rule (Governor's Rule): On Governor's report OR otherwise; for failure of constitutional machinery in state
cards.alert: ⭐ Governor = 'agent of Centre in state' (critics say) but constitution says Governor acts on CM's advice in most matters. Discretionary powers are most controversial aspect. 'Constitutional head' like President at Centre. Key difference: Governor has MORE discretion than President in some contexts (especially when no clear majority in assembly).

cards.heading: Supreme Court — Complete Study Notes
points: Art. 124: Establishment of Supreme Court — Parliament can increase judge strength by law; originally 8 (CJI + 7); currently 34 (CJI + 33)
Seat: Delhi (Art. 130); CJI can hold sittings at other places with President's approval
Appointment (Art. 124(2)): By President after consulting SC and HC judges (in practice = Collegium system)

Collegium System: No constitutional basis; judicial development (3 Judges Cases — SP Gupta 1982, SC Advocates on Record 1993, Presidential Reference 1998); Collegium = CJI + 4 senior-most SC judges; recommends appointments + transfers

NJAC (National Judicial Appointments Commission): 99th Amendment 2014 + NJAC Act 2014; declared unconstitutional by SC in 2015 (Fourth Judges Case) — violated Basic Structure (independence of judiciary)
Qualification: Must have been HC judge for 5 years OR advocate in HC for 10 years OR distinguished jurist in President's opinion

Removal (Art. 124(4)): By President on address of BOTH Houses of Parliament; each house must pass by: (i) majority of total membership + (ii) 2/3 majority of members present and voting; for proved misbehavior or incapacity; NO removal of SC judge in Indian history
Oath administered by: President

rows: Disputes between Centre and State(s); State vs State; exclusive and final; NOT disputes between individuals vs state
For enforcement of Fundamental Rights; issues 5 writs; right to move SC itself is a Fundamental Right
Appeals from HC in constitutional/civil/criminal matters; Special Leave Petition (Art. 136) — from any court/tribunal in India
President can refer question of law or fact to SC for opinion; SC opinion = advisory NOT binding on President
Maintains records of its proceedings; can punish for contempt of court
Can review its own judgments; suo motu powers

cards.alert: ⭐ Art. 136 — Special Leave Petition (SLP): Most powerful; SC can grant leave to appeal from ANY court/tribunal in India (including military tribunals). SC's original jurisdiction (Art. 131) = EXCLUSIVE for Centre vs State disputes. SC can transfer cases from one HC to another (Art. 139A).

cards.heading: High Courts — Structure, Powers and Jurisdiction
points: Art. 214: There shall be a High Court for each State; Parliament can establish common HC for 2 or more states (e.g., Punjab and Haryana HC)
Art. 216: HC consists of Chief Justice + such other judges as President thinks fit to appoint
Appointment (Art. 217): By President after consulting CJI, HC Chief Justice, and State Governor; Collegium system applies
Qualification: 10 years as advocate of HC OR 10 years as judicial officer in India
Removal (Art. 217(1)(b)): Same process as SC judges (address of Parliament with special majority) OR by proven incapacity

Jurisdiction of HC: Original jurisdiction (some HCs — Bombay, Calcutta, Madras, Delhi for company law, admiralty, matrimonial); Writ jurisdiction (Art. 226 — WIDER than SC; for FR violation + other legal rights); Appellate jurisdiction (from subordinate courts); Supervisory jurisdiction (Art. 227 — over all courts in state except SC); Power to transfer cases from one court to another in state

Art. 226 vs Art. 32: HC under Art. 226 can issue writs for BOTH FR violation AND other legal rights; SC under Art. 32 can issue writs ONLY for FR violation; HC territorial jurisdiction wider (can address any government official in state); but SC writ = enforcement across India

cards.heading: Important Judicial Doctrines for Exam

rows: Parliament cannot amend the Basic Structure of Constitution even through Art. 368
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) — 13-judge bench
Power of SC/HC to examine constitutionality of legislation and executive action
Marbury v. Madison (USA 1803); Art. 13 + Art. 32 + Art. 226 in India
If part of a law is unconstitutional, only that part is struck down (severed), not the whole law
Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950)
Pre-constitutional laws that violate FRs become dormant (eclipsed) not void; revived if FR is removed
Bhikaji Narain Dhakras v. State of MP (1955)
SC can overrule its previous decision but apply new ruling only to future cases (not past ones)
I.C. Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967)
Before depriving of FR, procedure must be right AND law itself must be fair, just, reasonable
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) — expanded Art. 21
Legislature cannot do indirectly what it cannot do directly; cannot evade constitutional limitations by changing form of law
KT Plantation Pvt. Ltd. v. State of Karnataka (2011)
When two provisions conflict, interpret them to give effect to both rather than rendering one otiose
Standard interpretation principle in Constitutional law
Privacy is a fundamental right under Art. 21 as part of right to life and personal liberty
Justice KS Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) — 9-judge bench
Right to die with dignity includes right to refuse medical treatment; advance medical directive (living will) valid
Common Cause v. Union of India (2018)

cards.alert: ⭐ Basic Structure components (not exhaustive): Supremacy of Constitution, Sovereign democratic republic, Secularism, Separation of powers, Federalism, Parliamentary system, Rule of law, Judicial review, Free and fair elections, Unity and integrity of India, Art. 32, Harmony between FR and DPSP, Limited power of Parliament to amend Constitution.

cards.heading: Public Interest Litigation (PIL) and Legal Aid
points: PIL: Any citizen can approach SC or HC for enforcement of public rights even if not personally aggrieved; liberalized locus standi (standing); started by Justice P.N. Bhagwati in early 1980s
First PIL: Mumbai Kamgar Sabha v. Abdul Thai (1976); S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (1981) = landmark; Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar (1979) = released undertrial prisoners
Written petition (even letter to SC) can be treated as PIL — Sunil Batra v. Delhi Administration (1978); letter from prisoners treated as writ petition

Limitations of PIL: Court cannot issue mandamus to legislate; cannot direct executive policy; cannot monitor ongoing compliance (court not administration); PIL abuse: vexatious PILs, business rivalries, publicity PILs
Lok Adalats: Alternative dispute resolution; governed by Legal Services Authorities Act 1987; no court fees; compromise settlement; award = decree of civil court; appeal to HC directly

Legal Services Authorities: National Legal Services Authority (NALSA); State Legal Services Authorities; Taluk Legal Services Committees; provide free legal aid to: women, children, SC/ST, persons with disability, disaster victims, persons in custody, industrial workmen, etc.
Art. 39A (DPSP): Equal justice and free legal aid — state must ensure equal justice is not denied due to poverty

cards.heading: Is India Federal? — Analysis
points: India is described as: 'quasi-federal' (K.C. Wheare); 'Federal with unitary bias' (Prof. K. Santhanam); 'A Union of States, not a federation' (Ambedkar in Constituent Assembly)
Art. 1: India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States — deliberately 'Union' not 'Federation'
FEDERAL FEATURES: Written constitution; supremacy of constitution; division of powers (3 lists); independent judiciary with power of judicial review; bicameral legislature; rigid constitution

UNITARY FEATURES: Strong Centre; single citizenship; single integrated judiciary; appointment of governors by Centre; emergency provisions that convert to unitary; Rajya Sabha not equal to Lok Sabha; residuary powers with Centre; All India Services under Centre's control; integrated audit machinery (CAG)
Cooperate Federalism: Emerging concept; GST Council (Art. 279A — 101st Amendment 2016) is best example; NITI Aayog promotes cooperative federalism; Inter-State Council (Art. 263)

cards.heading: Union, State and Concurrent Lists
points: 7th Schedule contains 3 lists dividing legislative powers between Centre and States

UNION LIST (List I): 100 subjects; ONLY Parliament can legislate; Defence, Atomic energy, Foreign affairs, Railways, Banking (except cooperative banks), Insurance, Currency, Post and telegraph, Census, Central universities, SC/HC judges, Citizenship, Immigration, Pirates and crimes on High Seas

STATE LIST (List II): 61 subjects; State Legislature primarily legislates (Parliament can in special circumstances); Law and order (Police), Agriculture, Public health and sanitation, Land and land reform, Fisheries, Local government (Panchayats+Municipalities), State public services, Betting and gambling, Cooperative societies

CONCURRENT LIST (List III): 52 subjects; BOTH Parliament and State can legislate; In case of conflict — CENTRAL LAW PREVAILS (Art. 254); Education (moved from State to Concurrent by 42nd Amendment 1976), Forests (moved from State to Concurrent by 42nd Amendment), Criminal law (IPC, CrPC), Civil procedure (CPC), Marriage and divorce, Bankruptcy, Trade unions, Drugs and poisons, Economic and social planning, Legal/Medical professions, Environmental protection
RESIDUARY POWERS (Art. 248): With Parliament (Union) — subjects not in any list; e.g., internet/cyber law, outer space, nuclear power = residuary (given to Centre unlike USA where with States)
cards.alert: ⭐ 42nd Amendment 1976 moved Education and Forests from State List to Concurrent List. GST: 101st Amendment 2016 inserted new Art. 246A giving BOTH Centre and States power to legislate on GST. Residuary power with Centre (unlike USA where with States, Germany where with Länder). In conflict on Concurrent List: Central law prevails UNLESS state law received Presidential assent before Central law (Art. 254(2)).

cards.heading: Legislative, Administrative and Financial Relations
points: Legislative: Parliament can legislate on State List in: national emergency (Art.352); President's Rule (Art.356); RS resolution by 2/3 majority in national interest (Art.249); when states request Parliament to legislate (Art.252)

Administrative: Central directions to states (Art.256, 257); All India Services — IAS/IPS officers serve both Centre and States but recruited and controlled by Centre; Governor's reports to President; disputes about river waters (Art.262)
Financial: Consolidated Fund of India + State Consolidated Funds; Centre can give grants to states; Finance Commission (Art.280) recommends sharing of taxes

Inter-state disputes: Art. 131 — dispute between states in SC; Art. 262 — dispute on inter-state rivers; River Water Disputes Act 1956; Inter-State River Water Disputes Tribunals (Cauvery, Krishna, Ravi-Beas disputes)
Interstate Council (Art. 263): Established by Presidential order 1990; PM as Chairman + all CMs; investigates and discusses disputes; recommends policies; meets annually

Sarkaria Commission (1983–88): Reviewed Centre-State relations; major recommendations: Governor should be non-politician + belong to other state + be consulted with CM before appointment; Art. 356 should be used sparingly; State List should not be eroded; Interstate Council should meet regularly; many not implemented
Punchhi Commission (2007–10): Updated Sarkaria Commission recommendations; emphasized cooperative federalism; recommended Independent body for Centre-State consultation

cards.heading: Finance Commission and GST Council
points: Finance Commission (Art. 280): Constitutional body; President constitutes it every 5 years (or earlier); Chairman + 4 members; quasi-judicial body

Functions: Recommends: (i) distribution of net proceeds of taxes between Centre and States (vertical devolution); (ii) allocation among states of respective shares (horizontal distribution); (iii) grants-in-aid to states; (iv) measures to augment state consolidated funds for panchayats and municipalities
16th Finance Commission (2023): N.K. Singh (also chaired 15th FC); covering period 2026–2031
15th Finance Commission (2021–26): N.K. Singh; recommended 41% vertical devolution to states (unchanged from 14th FC's 42% minus J&K UT); special grants to local bodies

GST Council (Art. 279A): Added by 101st Constitutional Amendment Act 2016; Joint forum of Centre + States; chaired by Union Finance Minister; Vice-Chairpersons = State Finance Ministers; decisions by 3/4 majority (Centre's vote = 1/3 weight; States' combined vote = 2/3 weight); BEST EXAMPLE of cooperative federalism
GST: Dual GST — CGST (Central) + SGST (State) on same transaction; IGST on inter-state; 4 GST Council rates: 5%, 12%, 18%, 28%; GST Network (GSTN) = IT backbone
cards.alert: ⭐ Finance Commission vs Planning Commission vs NITI Aayog: Finance Commission = constitutional (Art. 280); mandatory; distributes existing taxes. Planning Commission (abolished 2014) = non-constitutional; allocated funds through 5-year plans. NITI Aayog (2015) = non-constitutional; think tank; no fund allocation. GST Council = constitutional (after 101st Amendment); best example of cooperative federalism.

cards.heading: ECI — Structure, Powers and Functions
points: Art. 324: Superintendence, direction and control of elections to Parliament, state legislatures, offices of President and Vice President vested in Election Commission
Multi-member body since 1989 (made permanent by 61st Amendment): Chief Election Commissioner + 2 Election Commissioners
Originally (1950–1989) and again (during VP Singh government 1990) = single member ECI; currently = multi-member
Appointment: By President; no qualifications mentioned in Constitution; CEC and ECs have same powers and salary

Security of tenure: CEC cannot be removed except by impeachment process (like SC judge — special majority of Parliament); other Election Commissioners can be removed by President on recommendation of CEC
This asymmetry: CEC well protected; ECs NOT as well protected — concern about ECI independence
Model Code of Conduct (MCC): Comes into force with election announcement; NOT legally binding but compliance ensured by ECI; parties and government must follow it

Functions: Delimit constituencies (via Delimitation Commission); Voter roll preparation; Election schedule; Registration of political parties; Recognition of national + state parties; Allotment of election symbols; Election observers; EVM and VVPAT oversight; NOTA introduced 2013
cards.alert: ⭐ Chief Election Commissioners: Sukumar Sen (1st CEC; 1951 = first general election); T.N. Seshan (10th CEC; revolutionized ECI — photo ID cards, strict enforcement of MCC, de-recognition of parties). Current: Rajiv Kumar (25th CEC). ECI issues Model Code of Conduct — NO legal backing but powerful social/political force.

cards.heading: CAG — India's Supreme Audit Institution
points: Art. 148: There shall be a Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India
Appointed by: President; oath administered by President
Removal: Same process as SC judge — special majority of both houses of Parliament (most secure tenure in India?)
Salary: Charged to Consolidated Fund of India (not voted — secures independence)
After retirement: Cannot hold any further office under Government of India or State Government (to preserve integrity)

Functions: Audits ALL accounts of Union Government + State Governments; audits all bodies substantially financed by government; conducts performance audits + compliance audits + financial audits; submits reports to President (for Union) and Governor (for states)
Reports: Central audit reports go to President → laid before Parliament → scrutinized by Public Accounts Committee (PAC); state reports go to Governor → state legislature → state PAC
'Guardian of the public purse' and 'Watchdog of public finance'
cards.alert: ⭐ CAG audits: All government accounts + PSUs + aided bodies. NOT audits: Private companies (unless government has >51% stake). CAG cannot audit policy (whether policy is good/bad) — only whether money was spent as authorized and efficiently. Dr. V.V. Rao, V. Narahariah = famous CAGs. Vinod Rai (2008–13) = 2G Spectrum scam CAG report.

cards.heading: Union Public Service Commission (UPSC)
points: Art. 315: Public Service Commissions for the Union and States
Art. 316: Appointment by President; Chairman + such members; Chairman of UPSC cannot return to government employment after retirement
Art. 317: Removal — same as SC judge (special majority of Parliament) or by Supreme Court investigation for misbehavior
Salary: Charged to Consolidated Fund of India

Functions: Recruitment to civil services (IAS, IPS, IFS, Central Services Group A and B); advises on transfer, promotion, disciplinary matters; advises on claims for pension; consulted on matters relating to civil services
UPSC does NOT recruit: Military officers (UPSC recommends for some; SSBO for others); Railway Board staff; appointments below Group B; All India Services at state level (state PSCs handle)

UPSC exams: Civil Services Exam (IAS/IPS/IFS); Engineering Services; Medical Services; Combined Defence Services; NDA/NA; CAPF (Central Armed Police Forces); Combined Geo-Scientist; CBI Inspector; etc.
State PSCs (Art. 315): Each state has PSC; appointment by Governor; removal same as UPSC member; recruits for state services

cards.heading: Attorney General and Other Bodies

rows: Chief law officer of Government of India; highest law officer; appointed by President; must be qualified to be SC judge; not a government servant; right of audience in all courts in India; paid such remuneration as President determines
Second law officer; assists Attorney General; appointed by government
Safeguard interests of Scheduled Castes; monitor constitutional and other legal safeguards; investigate complaints; advise Union and state governments; report to President annually
Same as SC commission but for Scheduled Tribes; added by 89th Amendment 2003 (separated from combined commission)
Added by 102nd Amendment 2018 (Constitution Amendment); advises government on inclusion/exclusion of communities in OBC lists
National Commission for Minorities Act 1992; 5 religious minorities + 1 linguistic minority; safeguards interests of minority communities
Constituted every 5 years; distributes taxes between Centre and States
Appointed by President; investigates matters relating to linguistic minorities; submits reports to President

cards.alert: ⭐ Attorney General: Can participate in both Houses of Parliament but CANNOT vote; has right of audience in ALL courts in India. Solicitor General + Additional Solicitors General assist AG. AG holds office during pleasure of President (not protected tenure). Current AG: R. Venkataramani (appointed October 2022).

cards.heading: Types of Constitutional Amendments
points: Art. 368: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution

Type 1 — By Simple Majority (NOT under Art. 368): Admission of new states, formation of new states, change of boundaries; creation/abolition of Legislative Councils in states; citizenship; elections; parliamentary privileges; etc. — just like ordinary legislation
Type 2 — By Special Majority (Under Art. 368): 2/3 majority of members PRESENT AND VOTING in each house + majority of TOTAL MEMBERSHIP of each house; most amendments use this method

Type 3 — By Special Majority + Ratification by States: Art. 368 + must be ratified by MINIMUM HALF of state legislatures; for: provisions affecting federal structure — election of President (Art.54, 55), extent of executive power of Union/States, distribution of legislative powers, representation in Parliament, SC jurisdiction and powers, amendment procedure itself
Key: No provision for joint sitting in case of constitutional amendment — BOTH houses must independently pass the amendment with special majority
cards.alert: ⭐ 'Total membership' = full sanctioned strength (543 for LS even if seats vacant); 'members present and voting' = those actually there and voting. Special majority = BOTH conditions met simultaneously. No constitutional amendment can violate Basic Structure (Kesavananda Bharati 1973).

rows: Added 9th Schedule; inserted restrictions on freedom of speech (Art.19(2)); constitutional validity of land reform laws protected
Reorganisation of states on linguistic basis; abolished distinction between Part A, B, C states
Affirmed Parliament's power to amend any part of Constitution including FRs (response to Golaknath case)
Art. 31C added — DPSP on Art.39(b)(c) cannot be challenged for violating Art.14, 19; weakened FR-DPSP conflict

Most comprehensive; added Socialist, Secular, Integrity to Preamble; added Fundamental Duties (Art.51A); made DPSP superior to FRs; extended Emergency period; curtailed SC/HC powers; called 'mini-constitution'

Reversed some 42nd Amend. provisions; removed Right to Property from FRs (Art.300A); restored SC/HC powers; Arts. 20 and 21 cannot be suspended even in Emergency; changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion' for Art.352
Reduced voting age from 21 to 18 years (Art.326)
Added Part IX (Panchayati Raj) + 11th Schedule; 3-tier panchayat system; reservation for SC/ST/women
Added Part IXA (Urban Local Bodies/Municipalities) + 12th Schedule; elected ward committees
Added Art. 21A (Right to Education for 6–14 years as FR); changed Art. 45 (now early childhood care under 6); added 11th Fundamental Duty
Defection law strengthened; disqualified members cannot be ministers; CoM at Centre = max 15% of LS; states CoM = max 15% of state assembly
Added 'cooperative' to Art.19(1)(c); added Art.43B (cooperative societies as DPSP); Part IXB (cooperatives)
Established NJAC (National Judicial Appointments Commission) — struck down by SC in 2015
10% EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) reservation in education and public employment (Art.15(6), Art.16(6))
Abolished Anglo-Indian nomination to Parliament and state legislatures; extended SC/ST reservation for 10 more years (till 2030)
Restored states' power to identify OBCs; central and state lists of OBCs separate

cards.heading: Tricks to Remember Key Amendments
points: 42nd Amendment 1976: 'THE MOST AMENDED AMENDMENT' — called 'Mini-Constitution'; Indira Gandhi's Emergency era; added Socialist+Secular+Integrity; Fundamental Duties; Art.31C extended
44th Amendment 1978: 'RESTORATION AMENDMENT' — Janata Party reversed Emergency excesses; removed Property as FR; Arts 20+21 cannot suspend

TRICK for Amendment numbers: 1=Land reforms (9th Schedule); 7=States reorganised; 24=Parliament power to amend; 42=Mini constitution (1976 Emergency); 44=Restoration (1978); 52=Anti-defection (10th Schedule); 61=Vote at 18; 73=Panchayat; 74=Municipality; 86=RTE (education FR); 91=15% CoM cap; 101=GST; 103=EWS 10%
Schedules mnemonic: '1-Names, 2-Salaries, 3-Oaths, 4-Rajya Sabha seats, 5-ST areas, 6-NE tribes, 7-Three Lists, 8-Languages(22), 9-Land reforms, 10-Anti-defection, 11-Panchayat, 12-Municipality'
SOCIALIST+SECULAR added to Preamble: 42nd Amendment 1976 (Swaran Singh Committee recommended; Indira Gandhi implemented during Emergency)
Right to vote age reduced 21→18: 61st Amendment 1988 (Rajiv Gandhi era)
Right to Education as FR: 86th Amendment 2002 (NDA government; Atal Bihari Vajpayee era)

cards.heading: National Emergency — Complete Details
points: Grounds (Art. 352): War; External Aggression; Armed Rebellion (changed from 'internal disturbance' by 44th Amendment 1978 — higher bar)
Proclamation: By President on written advice of CABINET (not just PM — 44th Amendment safeguard; to prevent single person misuse)
Approval: By BOTH Houses of Parliament within ONE MONTH by Special Majority (2/3 majority present and voting + majority of total membership of each house)
Duration: 6 months; can be extended by 6 months each time with fresh parliamentary approval

Revocation: (i) President can revoke; (ii) Lok Sabha can pass resolution by SIMPLE MAJORITY (introduced by at least 1/10th of total LS members — about 55 members); Rajya Sabha has NO role in revocation

Effects on Centre-State relations: Parliament can legislate on State List; Centre can give directions to states on any matter; Art. 19 freedoms SUSPENDED; President's rule and state executives continue but under Centre's direction
Effects on FR: Art. 19 freedoms suspended automatically; Art. 20 and 21 CANNOT be suspended (44th Amendment); fundamental rights of citizens reduced significantly

National Emergency proclaimed THREE TIMES: 1962 (Sino-Indian War — Art.352; lasted till 1968); 1971 (Indo-Pak War — Art.352); 1975 (Internal disturbance — Indira Gandhi; controversial; lasted 1975–1977)
cards.alert: ⭐ 1975 Emergency: Proclaimed on 25 June 1975 by President Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed on PM Indira Gandhi's advice; reason = 'internal disturbance' (Allahabad HC found her election invalid); revoked March 1977 after electoral defeat. This led to 44th Amendment 1978 — changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion'; required Cabinet (not just PM) advice.

cards.heading: President's Rule — Art. 356 (State Emergency)
points: Grounds: 'Failure of constitutional machinery in a state' — President satisfied (on Governor's report OR otherwise) that government cannot be carried on in accordance with Constitution
Governor's role: Governor sends report to President recommending President's Rule; but President can also impose without Governor's report ('or otherwise')

Proclamation: By President; approved by BOTH Houses of Parliament within 2 months by SIMPLE MAJORITY (not special majority); can be extended beyond 1 year ONLY IF: national emergency is in operation OR EC certifies elections cannot be held in state
Maximum duration: 3 years (6 months × 6 times with parliamentary approval); CANNOT exceed 3 years without fresh constitutional amendment
Effect: State legislature dissolved (or suspended); President directly governs through Governor; High Court continues to function; state legislature powers exercised by Parliament

Judicial review (S.R. Bommai v. Union of India 1994): SC ruled President's Rule under Art.356 is subject to judicial review; if SC finds proclamation mala fide or based on irrelevant grounds = invalid; state government can be restored
Bommai case effect: After 1994, misuse of Art.356 declined significantly; 'imposing President's Rule has become much harder after Bommai case'
President's Rule imposed most: Uttar Pradesh (10 times); Manipur; Bihar; Kerala; Uttarakhand (courts struck down in 2016)
cards.alert: ⭐ S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994): 9-judge bench; most important case on federalism + Art.356; SC held President's Rule = SUBJECT TO JUDICIAL REVIEW; floor test must be held in assembly to determine majority, not Governor's personal assessment; laid down guidelines for imposition. Punchhi Commission (2010) recommended strict guidelines for Art.356 use.

cards.heading: Financial Emergency — Art. 360
points: Grounds: President satisfied that financial stability or credit of India (or any part thereof) is threatened
Approval: By both Houses of Parliament within 2 months by simple majority
Duration: No maximum period specified; continues until revoked
Effect: President can give financial directions to any state; can reduce salaries of persons serving Union including SC judges; can ask states to reserve money bills for President's consideration
NEVER IMPOSED in Indian history — no Financial Emergency has ever been proclaimed

1991 crisis: India faced severe balance of payments crisis (foreign exchange reserves = only 2 weeks of imports); Financial Emergency was considered but instead LPG reforms were implemented through democratic means
cards.alert: ⭐ Financial Emergency has NEVER been proclaimed in India's history. The 1991 BOP crisis (when India pledged 67 tons of gold to Bank of England/Bank of Japan as collateral) was the closest India came — but instead PM Narasimha Rao + FM Manmohan Singh chose LPG reforms. Financial Emergency provisions in Art.360.

cards.heading: Three Emergencies — Comparison Table

rows: Failure of constitutional machinery in state
Threat to financial stability or credit
President on Governor's report OR otherwise
Special Majority (2/3 + majority of total) within 1 MONTH
6 months; renewable with fresh approval; no maximum
President or LS by simple majority (1/10 members can introduce)
Parliament can legislate on State List; Governors take directions from Centre
State legislature dissolved/suspended; Parliament legislates for state
President can direct states on financial matters; reduce salaries

cards.alert: ⭐ KEY EXAM TRICK: National Emergency = SPECIAL majority + 1 MONTH; President's Rule and Financial Emergency = SIMPLE majority + 2 MONTHS. Art.356 = 'most abused constitutional provision' before Bommai (1994). Art.20 and 21 protection during National Emergency = added by 44th Amendment 1978 as safeguard against Emergency misuse.
